text:
# Page 1^page=1
Realization of a Shared Manufacturing Network using Capabilities, Skills and Services Marco Simon∗ Chris Urban†, Michael Winter‡ and Aljosha K¨ocher§, ∗Technologie-Initiative SmartFactory KL e.V. - Kaiserslautern, Germany Email: marco.simon@smartfactory.de †Institute for Automation Engineering - Otto-von-Guericke University - Magdeburg, Germany Email: chris.urban@ovgu.de ‡Chair of Information and Automation Systems - RWTH Aachen University - Aachen, Germany Email: m.winter@iat.rwth-aachen.de §Institute of Automation - Helmut-Schmidt University - Hamburg, Germany Email: aljosha.koecher@hsu-hh.de Abstract—Recent developments in Manufacturing-as-a-Service aim to create more flexible and resilient manufacturing networks by enabling the dynamic allocation of manufacturing tasks across company boundaries. This paper presents a pilot implementation of a shared manufacturing network based on the Capability, Skill and Service model and the Asset Administration Shell. The proposed approach supports semantically rich, platform- independent service descriptions and employs standardized nego- tiation logic based on the Industrie 4.0 language. A multi-partner prototype demonstrates how service requesters and providers – modeled as proactive AAS instances – can autonomously negoti- ate manufacturing services using capability and offer submodels. The implementation comprises cross-partner negotiation and feasibility checks, skill-based execution, and dynamic scheduling are handled individually at the partner level. Our findings highlight the benefits and challenges of integrating capabilities, skills and services with Asset Administration Shells standards in shared manufacturing scenarios and provide a foundation for future extensions toward more expressive interaction protocols and scalable, federated manufacturing ecosystems. Index Terms—Shared Manufacturing, Manufacturing as a Service, Capabilities, Services, Skills I. INTRODUCTION Recent global crises—such as the COVID-19 pandemic, shortages in critical components, and increasing regulatory pressures like climate neutrality targets and supply chain due diligence laws—have exposed the fragility of traditional, rigid global supply chains. A key competitive advantage in the future will lie in the resilience of supply chains, particularly the ability to react quickly and flexibly to unforeseen events [1]. Manufacturing-as-a-Service (MaaS) offers a promising solution by enabling dynamic, ad hoc collaboration among manufacturing partners through digital platforms [2]. By in- tegrating external manufacturing capacities and automating order distribution across factory and company boundaries, MaaS enables cost-efficient customization, rapid prototyping, decentralized manufacturing, and more sustainable, responsive supply chains [3]. This paper presents a pilot implementation of a distributed MaaS network developed by a consortium of German research institutions. The primary goal is to establish a federated, interoperable manufacturing ecosystem that allows for the dynamic allocation of manufacturing resources across organizational and geographical boundaries. The approach leverages key concepts from Industry 4.0, such as the Capa- bility, Skill and Service (CSS) model introduced in [4] and the Asset Administration Shell (AAS), to enable flexible, scalable, and manufacturer-independent collaboration. In contrast to existing commercial platforms, which often rely on proprietary descriptions, centralized architectures and manual integration efforts, the proposed solution emphasizes decentralization and semantic interoperability. Orders can be created, routed, and processed across multiple sites with minimal manual inter- vention, thereby reducing transaction costs and unlocking manufacturing capacities. More specifically, we aim to answer the following research questions: • How can the CSS model be applied to enable flexible and semantically consistent collaboration in shared manufac- turing networks? • What architectural and organizational mechanisms are required to implement and coordinate a multi-partner MaaS ecosystem? The remainder of this paper is structured as follows: Sec- tion II introduces core concepts of the CSS model as the foundation of our approach. Section III contains an analysis of related work. Section IV outlines the architecture and design principles of the proposed shared manufacturing network. Section V presents the implementation details and partner- specific contributions. Section VI summarizes key findings and limitations, while Section VII concludes the paper and outlines directions for future research. II. CSS MODEL The CSS model was developed in the context of Industry 4.0 to enable greater flexibility, modularity, and interoperability in manufacturing systems. It provides a formal, technology- independent approach to describe the functions (a) required by manufacturing process descriptions and (b) offered by

# Page 2^page=2
manufacturing resources, enabling their application in dynamic and distributed manufacturing networks. The CSS model ex- tends the well-established product, process and resource (PPR) paradigm by introducing the following core concepts: • Capability: An implementation-independent specifica- tion of a manufacturing-related function [5]. Provided capabilities define what a resource is functionally able to perform, e.g., “drilling a hole with a diameter ≤ 10 mm in steel”. Required capabilities are used to express pro- cess requirements. • Service: A service packages one or more capabilities and adds organizational and commercial data, such as cost, delivery time, or certification [5]. Services are essential for exposing capabilities in inter-organizational scenarios, e.g., in shared manufacturing environments. • Skill: The concrete, executable implementation of a ca- pability on a specific resource [5]. Skills expose control- level information such as parameters and state machines via a so-called skill interface, allowing integration into automation systems. The CSS model was originally introduced by Plattform Industrie 4.0 [5] and later extended in [6], where methods for describing, assigning, and deriving capabilities were in- troduced. These engineering methods aim to provide abstract guidelines in improving the practical applicability of the CSS model in order to facilitate its integration into digital twins and smart factory systems. III. RELATED WORK 1) Asset Administration Shell (AAS): A core concept in Industry 4.0 developments is the AAS. The AAS is an In- dustry 4.0 specification for a digital twin, aiming to improve interoperability across systems from different manufacturers. Published specifications define a standardized AAS metamodel and corresponding interfaces [7]. The metamodel includes a set of elements known as Submodel Elements (SMEs), which are used to create I4.0-compliant information models. These SMEs are grouped into Submodel Element Collections (SMCs) and further organized into Submodels (SMs), which represent specific aspects and functionalities of an asset. The use of standardized submodels is a key step toward cross- company interoperability. Furthermore, the Plattform Industrie 4.0 initiative classifies AAS interaction patterns into three types. Type 1 – file-based - AAS are manually edited and exchanged data contents. Type 2 – API-based AAS - provides various interfaces for automated information exchange. Type 3 AAS are capable of autonomous, message-based communi- cation with other participants [7]. 2) I4.0 language: Communication between the participants in the network is described using the I4.0 language and the tendering procedure defined in VDI 2193 [8, 9]. VDI Guideline 2193 defines a three-part rule set for the I4.0 lan- guage. The structure of messages and the valid vocabulary are specified in VDI 2193-1. As shown on the left side in Figure 1, each message consists of a frame and an interaction element. The frame includes attributes for both identification (e.g., sender ID, receiver ID) and contextualization (e.g., message type, conversation ID). The interaction element is derived from the AAS metamodel and represents any metamodel element, such as a submodel or submodel element. Another essential component of the I4.0 language is the set of interaction protocols. These define the sequence of messages exchanged during a dialogue between two or more I4.0 components. One predefined interaction protocol is the bidding procedure described in VDI 2193-2 as demonstrated on the right side in Figure 1. Based on the Contract Net Protocol of the Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents (FIPA), this procedure specifies the sequence of messages required to negotiate a service between a service requester and a service provider. However, the bidding protocol only specifies the types of messages that must be used, as well as the order in which they should be sent. It does not specify which elements of the AAS metamodel should be used for interaction elements, since this varies depending on the use case. Fig. 1. Structure of an Industry 4.0 message and a corresponding interaction protocol for bidding between a Service Requester and a Service Provider [8] 3) Module Type Package (MTP): The Module Type Pack- age (MTP) standard enables a formalized and vendor-neutral specification of interfaces, supporting both (a) manufacturing- related interactions through modular automation units and (b) interoperability between heterogeneous control systems and automation layers. As such, MTP supports designing mod- ular, adaptable, and scalable manufacturing infrastructures. By leveraging MTP, engineering efforts specific to individual projects can be significantly reduced while simultaneously promoting the reuse and modularization of automation com- ponents. This is achieved through the decoupled development of a Process Equipment Assembly (PEA) and the application of vendor-independent integration principles, as outlined in VDI/VDE/NAMUR 2658 [10]. At its core, MTP defines the functional behavior of PEAs, with a primary emphasis on the encapsulation and invocation of services. These services may be realized via distinct operation modes, depending on the module’s internal logic. For example, a “Stirring” service might be implemented either as a time-constrained

# Page 3^page=3
operation — executing for a predetermined duration — or as a continuous, indefinitely running action. Such variants are formally defined within the MTP as procedures. In addition to the description of services and their procedures, MTP also specifies the associated execution parameters required by the PEA. The actual control logic remains implemented in the programmable logic controller (PLC) of a PEA. In alignment with the CSS model [5], the functions implemented in the PLC are referred to as skills, whereas the services and procedures as described in the MTP, correspond to the module’s capabilities. 4) Other Approaches to Shared Manufacturing: The work on distributed manufacturing, [11] proposes a Web-based ap- proach for sharing manufacturing services such as simulation, fabrication, and testing. The approach encapsulates services in interactive web documents but lacks formal models for capabilities or negotiation logic. In contrast, our work builds on a standardized, semantically rich information model (CSS) to support interoperable service description and structured coordination in shared manufacturing networks. In [12] requirements for a manufacturing ontology are formulated. On this basis, it is examined which key con- cepts are already available in the existing literature to cover these requirements. The concepts identified are grouped into three thematic areas: Engineering Functions, Capabilities & Capacities and Resource Modelling. As noted in [12] the relationships between these concepts have been presented inconsistently and with little transparency in the literature. Therefore, an integrated ontological model is proposed that systematically brings these concepts together. It is based on the DOLCE ontology [13] and existing extensions. In addition, three specialized extensions Dolce+Functions, DolceEng and Dolce+Resources are introduced. The aim of these exten- sions is to map the previously defined requirements within a consistent framework. For this purpose, formal axioms and definitions are established in the paper. Finally, the extended ontological model is validated using a realistic example. In [2], Balta et al. propose a cloud-based manufacturing as a service framework to match small-batch manufacturing requests with underutilized resources. The system abstracts manufacturing steps into service requests and applies opti- mization algorithms to select manufacturers based on cost, time, and quality. Unlike our work, which builds on the CSS reference model and a precise interaction logic, the focus of [2] lies on system architecture and optimization logic, with less emphasis on formal capability descriptions. IV. SHARED MANUFACTURING BASED ON CSS The key objective of a MaaS architecture in a cross- enterprise context is to enable heterogeneous actors to au- tonomously negotiate manufacturing services. Within this negotiation process, two abstract roles can be identified: the Service Requester (SR), which articulates a material or immaterial demand through specific requirements; and the Service Provider (SP), which satisfies this demand by provid- ing appropriate capabilities. Implementing MaaS in an inter- organizational application scenario requires a high level of interoperability. Achieving this interoperability necessitates compliance with a variety of technical, syntactic, semantic, and organizational agreements among the involved actors [14]. From an analysis of relevant design aspects, the following key requirements can be derived: • Digital Representation: A digital representation in the form of a Digital Twin is essential since all interactions between participating assets, e.g. machines, smart facto- ries or products, take place virtually. The Digital Twin serves as the foundation for machine-readable interaction and data-driven decision-making processes. The standard- ization of the internal structure of the digital twin and its link to the asset is not necessarily a requirement for the success of MaaS. However, communication via external interfaces must be enabled between them. • Service Description: Both SR and SP must share a common structural and semantic understanding of the service. In the context of MaaS, this refers to a capability- centric service description, independent of specific tech- nical implementations. In terms of the CSS model this description must represent both technical and economic properties. • Information Exchange Mechanisms: To support equitable and efficient negotiations, actors must be able to exchange structured information reliably. This requires standardized formats and interaction sequences. Ideally, such exchange should be technology- and platform-neutral to ensure maximum system flexibility. • Infrastructure: A core prerequisite for MaaS is the abil- ity to identify suitable partners quickly. This includes mechanisms for provider pre-selection based on defined requirements, as well as discovery and matching services. Furthermore, fundamental IT requirements such as infor- mation security, confidentiality, availability, integrity, and authenticity must be addressed. An architecture based on proactive AAS as shown in Fig- ure 3, communicating via the standardized Industrie 4.0 (I4.0) language, is well-suited to fulfill the core requirements of a cross-enterprise MaaS environment. On the southbound inter- face, i.e. the interface of the AAS to the associated asset, AASs offer sufficient openness to integrate heterogeneous physical or digital assets. AASs support a broad range of control logic implementations, from lightweight finite state machines to complex agent-based decision architectures, thereby ensuring the required flexibility for dynamic manufacturing scenarios. On the northbound interface, i.e. the interface between the AAS and higher-level software systems, AASs promote inter- operability by employing modular and semantically grounded submodels that describe an asset’s capabilities and service requirements in a machine-readable format. In particular, the Capability Description submodel, currently under development by the Industrial Digital Twin Association (IDTA), is a promising candidate for the domain-independent representation of offered or required capabilities. To transform a technical capability into a service, additional commercial

# Page 4^page=4
Fig. 2. Sequence of I4.0 messages in CSS pilot attributes must be provided. This requires the use of a further submodel that captures relevant economic and contractual aspects. To date, no standardized submodel fulfilling this role is known to be under development within the IDTA. Therefore, in the context of the pilot implementation, the so-called Offer submodel - developed by the SmartFactory-KL (SF KL) - is employed to model the commercial aspects of the service. This submodel encapsulates essential commercial properties necessary for describing services. These include the price, delivery time, delivery modality, and payment conditions. The I4.0 language has been specifically designed to be com- patible with the AAS metamodel. Both the I4.0 language and the AAS use JSON serialization formats, ensuring structural compatibility and enabling submodels to be directly included within I4.0 messages. It includes two negotiation protocols: a basic bidding interaction and an iterative protocol for more complex service negotiation processes. The expressiveness of the Industrie 4.0 (I4.0) language depends on the use of appropriate submodels. These submodels serve as domain- specific vocabularies that define the semantics required for bidding interaction. For the CallForProposal message, the Capability Description submodel - from the requester’s perspective - is used to model the requirements of a needed capability as shown in Figure 2. In response, the Proposal message references the Offer submodel, which encodes the commercial and logistical terms of the proposed service. Currently, no dedicated InteractionElements are defined for additional message types. However, the I4.0 language design allows for such extensions. Defining further InteractionEle- ments could enhance the expressiveness and granularity of negotiations, especially in more complex service orchestration scenarios. Since the language itself is technology-agnostic, it remains independent of the underlying transport protocol. For the pilot implementation, the Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT) protocol was selected due to its simplicity and wide availability. MQTT is a communication protocol based on the publish/subscribe mechanism. Publishers send messages to specific topics, while subscribers listen to those topics to receive messages. A central broker is responsible for rout- ing messages between publishers and subscribers. To enable message exchange according to the I4.0 language within the Shared Manufacturing network, it is necessary to define which topics are used for communication between Service Requesters and Service Providers. A distinction must be made between public messages, such as the CallForProposal, which are intended to be received by all participants, and private messages containing sensitive information, such as Proposal and other message types defined by the Bidding Protocol. The most straightforward implementation would involve all participants publishing to and subscribing from a single shared topic. However, this approach makes private messages visible to all nodes in the network, which is undesirable. To address this, the Registry Infrastructure Component (RIC) is introduced, as proposed in [15], to organize MQTT topics and manage the dynamic routing of I4.0-compliant messages between AAS instances. Each AAS instance in the Shared Manufacturing network registers with the RIC by providing a descriptor that includes its AAS ID. After registration, it subscribes to a unique topic derived from its name and publishes all outbound messages to a static topic called AASpillarbox. The RIC monitors this shared topic, analyzes the message frame to determine the recipient, and then forwards the message to the corresponding topic of the intended AAS. V. IMPLEMENTATION The I4.0 language specification does not prescribe the type of asset that may act as a SR. The asset can be either a physical asset - such as a machine with maintenance requirements - or a virtual one, such as a product instance initiated by a customer order. In this pilot, a virtual product asset acts as the SR: a liquid hand soap tailored to individual customer preferences. These preferences include attributes such as fragrance, color, and the shape of the packaging container. Customers config- ure their desired product via a user interface, which serves as the entry point to the manufacturing network. Once the customer submits an order, a new AAS instance is created based on a predefined template. This template contains a state

# Page 5^page=5
machine that describes the internal manufacturing workflow. The instantiated AAS thus acts as a proactive SR within the network. The AAS is implemented using the PyAASxServer frame- work 1, which enables the creation of state-machine-driven AAS instances compliant with the current AAS meta-model specification. These AASs support both a REST API for the communication with the customer Web UI- for the generation of required submodels from the customer’s order data - and a communication interface using the I4.0 language. The latter allows the Service Requester to initiate the bidding process by issuing a CallForProposal message as soon as the order is confirmed. Communication within the network is handled via MQTT. Each AAS - both SR and SP - is assigned a dedicated topic based on its AAS ID. A central RIC is responsible for routing I4.0 language messages by checking the receiver field and forwarding them to the appropriate topic. This requires each participating AAS to be registered with the RIC. In the following subsections, each pilot partner describes the implementation of their specific SP and the skill-based integration of the respective manufacturing plants, which con- tributes one or more capabilities to the shared manufacturing of the individualized hand soap, see Figure 3. A. Partner A This implementation is based on the results of the Modular Smart Manufacturing GAIA-X test environment (SmartMa-X) [16] project. In this project, a product configurator was devel- oped with which a model truck can be individually configured and ordered. The ordering process initiated coordinated com- munication between different so-called manufacturing islands. A manufacturing island represents an example of a manufac- turing company. The interactions were modeled using AAS and Industry 4.0 messages and are documented in the form of sequence diagrams in [17]. This preliminary work formed the basis for the development of the use case presented here. As part of the implementation, a service provider was realized that is able to receive and interpret CallForProposal message based on the I4.0 communication model from the SR via MQTT. The InteractionElement contained in the I4.0 message corresponds to the Capability Description submodel of the AAS, in which the capabilities required by the service requester are described. The relevant information is extracted from the received submodel and compared with the available resource capabilities of the manufacturing islands. If the message is not compliant at this point, a response of type notUnderstood is sent to the SR. The capability specifications of the machines, which are also described in the Capability Description submodel, are stored in a repository together with other AAS submodels. The required submodels are retrieved from the repository via a REST interface (HTTP GET). Matching then takes place at the individual property level between the capabilities required by the service requester 1https://github.com/harishpakala/PythonAASxServer, accessed May 2025 and those offered by the service provider. If a corresponding capability could be found, a reference to the underlying skill is called up via a relation within the AAS of the resource. The skill model is represented by OPC UA objects. A separate nodeset for describing skills was developed. The structure of a skill follows three basic functional components: Feasibili- tyCheck, PreconditionCheck and SkillExecution. If a suitable capability has been identified, a FeasibilityCheck, described in [18] is carried out to ensure the basic feasibility at the level of the associated skills. The results of this check, such as the expected execution time or the anticipated costs, are summarized in an offer. This offer is sent to the SR within an I4.0 message as an interaction element with the type proposal. In the case of a positive response, i.e. acceptance of the offer by the service requester, the PreconditionCheck is then carried out. The PreconditionCheck assesses whether the skill can be executed under the current conditions. The actual execution then takes place in the SkillExecution phase. Each of these three components is equipped with a state machine whose structure is based on the OPC 30050 specification (PackML - Packaging Machine Language) 2. This enables a standardized and modular mapping of skills within an OPC UA-based architecture [18]. B. Partner B In process industry, modular automation offers an approach to achieve production flexibility. Within a shared manufac- turing network, process steps are executed by invoking MTP procedures provided by distributed resources. The mapping of requested services to offered capabilities of resources is facilitated by the Capability Description submodel of the AAS. These capabilities are subsequently composed into a master recipe, orchestrating the execution of the requested services. To realize such flexible production, the open-source process orchestration layer (POL) implementation Recipol3, developed by the Chair of Information and Automation Systems (IAT) at RWTH Aachen University, is employed. Recipol supports executing standardized process descriptions and utilizes the CSS model. Upon receiving a CallForProposal from an SR, an internal feasibility check is conducted at the IAT as SP. If the CallForProposal is either unclear or not feasible, a corresponding response is sent back to the SR. If the CallForProposal is feasible, the SP responds with a Proposal. The SR then chooses to either accept or reject the proposal. Upon acceptance, an internal scheduling process is initiated, defining the sequence of process steps by providing a master recipe. A central feature of Recipol is the execution of master recipes defined in BatchML 4, in compliance with the ISA-88 standard [19]. These recipes leverage capabilities provided by 2https://reference.opcfoundation.org/PackML/v101/docs/, accessed May 2025 3https://github.com/rwth-iat/Recipol/, accessed Apr. 2025 4https://github.com/MESAInternational/B2MML-BatchML, accessed Apr. 2025

# Page 6^page=6
RIC MQTT-Broker Service Requester Asset OPC UA Decision-making Component (FSM) Feasibility Check Scheduling Skill Execution Capability Matching :Filtration :Mixing ... Offer Submodel Asset SP - Partner C: Shared Manufacturing Network I4.0 Language Decision-making Component (FSM) Production Sequence Proposal Assessment :Filtration :Milling :Pulsing ... Service Requester (SR): I4.0 Language Capability Submodel (Requirements) Capability Submodel (Assurances) Service Provider (SP): Asset SP - Partner D: Asset SP - Partner A: Asset SP - Partner B: Fig. 3. Architecture of a Shared Manufacturing Network Based on Asset Administration Shells PEAs, whose skills are implemented via services and proce- dures described within the MTP file. At IAT, modular units known as Honeycomb act as PEAs, each providing distinct capabilities such as heating or mixing. These functionalities are implemented on PLCs from different vendors. To execute skills, Recipol parses the master recipe, identifies suitable PEAs, and coordinates their invocation in alignment with the required MTP procedures and services within the shared manufacturing network. C. Partner C As part of the pilot implementation, the Otto-von-Guericke University Magdeburg (OvGU) contributes a SP for fused deposition modeling - an additive manufacturing technique - within the manufacturing network. The SP is implemented based on the concept of finite state machines using the open- source PyAASFramework. A two-stage feasibility check is employed to evaluate whether the requirements of the re- quested service can be fulfilled: • In the first stage, a fast approximate feasibility check compares the Capability Description submodels of the service requester and service provider. The properties of both submodels are mapped using their semanticIDs. This stage addresses the check of basic constraints such as material availability, print space requirements and fulfillment of the requested parameters, e.g. layer height. • In the second stage, a more detailed skill-based feasi- bility check is performed. This step invokes domain- specific software components to assess process feasibility, considering parameters such as print stability, required print time, and filament consumption. It provides a higher prediction accuracy at the cost of increased computational effort. The underlying skills required for the second-stage evalua- tion are realized via interfaces to existing tools, including PrusaSlicer for toolpath generation, Fluidd for print job management, and Spoolman for filament tracking. These inte- grations enable the service provider to not only represent its capabilities semantically but also to execute concrete planning and decision-making tasks autonomously. After successful completion of the two-stage feasibility check, a manufacturing schedule is generated based on the estimated printing duration and the current job queue. This scheduling mechanism ensures that service offers reflect realistic availability and resource planning. By combining the time estimation with the pre- dicted filament consumption, a structured service offer can be composed as Proposal and returned to the SR in response to the original service inquiry. During service execution, the operational status of the 3D printer is continuously monitored via the integrated skill interfaces, which allows the SP to track the job progress. After successful completion of the service, the provider automatically notifies the staff to remove the component and check that the quality criteria are met. D. Partner D The approach to CSS that was taken by HSU places ontologies at the core of capability and skill modeling. As a foundational step, an ontology representing the CSS model was developed 5 and subsequently extended using relevant standards to enable a detailed and structured description of both capabilities and their associated skills6. This extended ontology allows for the specification of manufacturing ca- pabilities, including their properties and constraints, as well as representing skills and skill interfaces with all execution- relevant information. 5https://github.com/caskade-Automation/CSS 6https://github.com/caskade-Automation/CaSkMan

# Page 7^page=7
To support efficient ontology development, automated meth- ods have been created to transform existing engineering arti- facts into formal ontological models. In addition, generative approaches based on Large Language Models (LLMs) have been explored to derive capability ontologies from unstruc- tured plain-text descriptions [20]. For the technical realization of skills, multiple toolkits are provided. These support implementation in various environ- ments, including Java and Python, IEC 61131-compliant PLC programming, and the transformation of existing MTPs into structured capability and skill definitions. To enable interoperability with the AAS, a bidirectional mapping was developed that automatically translates between ontology-based capability descriptions and AAS submod- els [21]. This mapping ensures consistency and semantic align- ment between the ontological layer and the I4.0-compliant digital representation layer. The proposed approach has been applied to multiple use cases, including discrete manufacturing systems, process engi- neering plants, autonomous mobile robots, and purely compu- tational functions. In the pilot scenario of this paper, capabili- ties are offered for both the manufacturing of soap containers and the manufacturing and filling of the soap itself. When a CallForProposal containing a required ca- pability as an AAS is received, it is first translated into the abovementioned ontologies before a planning process is started. This planning process uses Satisfiability Modulo Theories to determine whether the required capability can be realized using one or more available provided capabilities [22]. All constraints specified in the request are considered, and only valid combinations of provided capabilities that jointly fulfill the request are selected. If no such combination can be found, a Refuse message is issued. Otherwise, the combination of suitable capabilities is translated from the ontological repre- sentation to an AAS and a Proposal is returned to the SR. Upon AcceptProposal, the capabilities identified in the planning process are executed through their associated skills. For orchestrating these skill executions, a BPMN-based workflow engine is employed, enabling dynamic coordination of the manufacturing process. VI. DISCUSSION The developed pilot demonstrates a complete application of the CSS model, covering capabilities, skills, and services in a distributed manufacturing scenario. Service requests were matched to capabilities across multiple partners and led to the actual execution of skills – confirming the practical fea- sibility of CSS-based interactions. The use of MQTT for decentralized communication proved beneficial. Public topics were used for broadcast messages like CallForProposal, while dedicated topics ensured secure and scalable delivery of sensitive messages. Nonetheless, some limitations remain. The SR lacks autonomous planning logic and a process must currently be configured manually. The RIC, as a central component, requires trusted hosting and redundancy to ensure availability. Additionally, the network supports only a star topology: a SP can only act as a spoke and cannot forward requests or delegate services to other (subordinated) SPs. While sufficient for the prototype, future work should explore horizontal and vertical SP collaboration and delegation to enable more flexible orchestration across shared manufacturing networks. VII. CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK This paper presented a pilot implementation of a shared MaaS network based on the CSS model and the AAS. The pro- posed approach enables semantically rich, machine-readable service descriptions and supports structured negotiation be- tween autonomous participants using the standardized Indus- trie 4.0 language. A multi-partner prototype demonstrated the feasibility of this approach across diverse manufacturing domains and control platforms. While the pilot illustrates the potential of CSS-based service interaction, several limitations remain. First, the current evaluation is limited to proof-of- concept implementations in controlled lab environments. More systematic evaluation under realistic industrial conditions is necessary to assess scalability, robustness, and performance. Second, although core components were implemented by dif- ferent partners, reuse across projects remains low. A modular developer toolkit would help reduce integration effort and accelerate adoption by third parties. Lastly, several real-world aspects—such as fault tolerance, security, compliance, and integration with legacy systems—were deliberately abstracted away in this early prototype and must be addressed to en- able industrial-scale deployment. Future work will focus on expanding the evaluation framework, improving reuse and extensibility through toolkit development, and incorporating non-functional requirements relevant for real-world applica- tion. Moreover, ongoing standardization efforts, particularly around capability and offer submodels, will be closely tracked to ensure alignment with emerging Industrie 4.0 ecosystems. ACKNOWLEDGMENT This work was partially supported by the German Ministry for Economic Affairs and Climate Action by the project “Aufbau eines Datenraums f¨ur die ausr¨ustende Industrie – die Ausr¨uster von Fabriken weltweit” (Factory-X) under grant 13MX001ZU.
